edge: DOLG proposed a method of fusion of local and global features of an image.
The geometric structure has been mainly considered within the re-ranking stage, while the global embedding hardly reflects the structure of the image.
Global embeddings are easy to be fooled by look-alike distractors with similar visual patterns even if their shapes are different, ultimately bringing down the overall retrieval performance.

node_2: representative visual properties and internal structures
edge: The proposed global embedding network aims to help representative visual properties and internal structures reflect well simultaneously in global embedding.
Self-similarity is a structural representation of an image, indicating how similar a specific part of an image is to the entire image or its neighborhood region.
Self-similarity was mainly used for two purposes, including self-similarity descriptor, which uses self-similarity as a robust local descriptor.

The self-similarity descriptor is a classical image descriptor developed to robustly match images of the same content under different photometric conditions such as lighting changes, color variance, texture differences, or even domain variances.
self-similarity descriptor has been used in various fields such as image matching, visual correspondence, few-shot classification, and video action recognition.
Recent approaches extract self-similarity descriptors from the intermediate feature map of CNN to help the network learn robust local and global representation.

In this paper, inspired by the previous works, we revisit this self-similarity descriptor in terms of convolution and propose a self-similarity encoder that embeds structural properties in global embeddings while learning diverse structures within numerous images.
Visual properties and geometric structures are valuable clues in image retrieval.

Thanks to the powerful proposal modules, the proposed network is not easily fooled by distractors with similar appearances.
The proposed network more accurately finds exact matches that match both visual properties and geometric structures.
SENet introduces a global embedding network in contrast to the conventional local self-similarity descriptor for image retrieval.
The global embedding network aims to reflect representative information of an image to global embedding for efficient matching in image retrieval tasks.
Global embedding networks achieve the stated purpose by extracting the intermediate feature map of CNNs and pooling it into global embedding.

The feature map F is aggregated into global embedding z through a global pooling operation, where global pooling operations naturally discard spatial information, affecting the structural cues in the global embedding.
The global embedding z hardly reflects the structural information about the input image, which is considered an invaluable cue for image matching.
This paper focuses on making the global embedding better reflect structural information while preserving the original visual information.
The SSC module computes pixel-wise self-similarity S to extract structural information inside the feature map F.
The SSC module transforms the intermediate feature map F to its projection F′ through a single linear layer to reduce computation complexity and add non-linearity to the original feature.
Inside the projected feature map F′, channel-wise non-negative self-similarity S is computed for every pixel position x and its surrounding region of size P × P using cosine similarity.

SSE encodes high-dimensional self-similarity into compact self-similarity descriptors while learning and analyzing diverse geometric structures from various images. D is gradually encoded from the dense channel-wise local self-similarity S.
SSE is constructed with a convolution block sequence comprising 3×3 convolution along the self-similarity dimension side, batch normalization layer, and ReLU function.

SSE encodes high-dimensional self-similarity into compact self-similarity descriptors, and FFM fuses self-similarity descriptors and the original feature map, to reflect both visual and structural information without breaking the original behavior of the base network.

The analysis discusses conducting ablation studies on input feature layer to determine the effectiveness of extracting structural information from different layers.
The ablation studies show that extracting structural information from the conv5 layer performs better than the conv4 layer in terms of higher performance.
The analysis compares the performance of extracting structural information from conv5 layer with higher performance than from the conv4 layer.
Despite conv5 having lower resolution than conv4, it enables the network to embed meaningful self-similarity by exploiting high-level semantic features.
The intermediate feature map from conv5 layer is used to extract internal self-similarities in the analysis.

Our proposed network still has weaknesses against the scale and the structural changes caused by image resolution and viewpoints.
To solve the weaknesses, we design an end-to-end learnable self-similarity encoder.
The self-similarity encoder learns various structures from various images.
Our future work aims to design a model that can enhance the consensus between the same content images with large scale/viewpoint differences.
The framework leverages the internal structures of images to reflect structural information well in global embeddings.
First, the framework proposes the self-similarity encoding module, which analyzes the internal structures of images.
The framework also proposes the feature fusion module to fuse visual and structural information harmoniously.
The significant performance improvements on several representative benchmarks demonstrate the value of internal structures of images for image retrieval.
